Pre-collected diagnostic snapshot The following data has already been gathered for every server in the cluster. Analyse it to identify issues, then use the available tools to investigate further if needed. { "server-c1": { "files": "entrypoint.sh server-c1.json", "entrypoint.sh": "#!/bin/sh set -e envsubst '${SERVER_ID} ${CLUSTER} ${MESSAGE}' \ < /etc/nginx/templates/default.conf.template \ > /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf nginx -g 'daemon off;' & NGINX_PID=$! sleep 2 # Append invalid syntax — nginx keeps serving from its loaded config, # but nginx -t will now report a syntax error printf '\n# corrupted entry\nserver {\n listen 9090\n invalid_directive\n' \ >> /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf wait $NGINX_PID ", "ls -la /app/logs/server-c1": "total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 May 9 14:08 . drwxr-xr-x 6 root root 4096 May 12 01:55 .. lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 access.log -> /dev/stdout lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 error.log -> /dev/stderr ", "GET /status": "HTTP 200 — {"server_id":"server-c1","cluster":"C","hostname":"server-c1","app_status":"ok","message":"I am a cluster C server!","nginx":{"version":"1.26.2","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}", "GET /app": "HTTP 404 — 404 Not Found

404 Not Found

nginx/1.26.2
" }, "server-c2": { "files": "entrypoint.sh server-c2.json", "entrypoint.sh": "#!/bin/sh set -e # Remove Docker's log symlinks so nginx writes to a real file on disk rm -f /var/log/nginx/error.log /var/log/nginx/access.log # Quoted heredoc — $hostname and $nginx_version are NOT expanded by the shell # and pass through literally for nginx to resolve at request time cat > /tmp/nginx.tmpl << 'TMPL' upstream app_backend { server 127.0.0.1:3000; } server { listen 80; server_name _; default_type application/json; error_log /var/log/nginx/error.log warn; access_log /var/log/nginx/access.log; location /status { return 200 '{"server_id":"${SERVER_ID}","cluster":"${CLUSTER}","hostname":"$hostname","app_status":"ok","message":"${MESSAGE}","nginx":{"version":"$nginx_version","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}'; } location /app { proxy_pass http://app_backend; proxy_connect_timeout 2s; proxy_read_timeout 2s; proxy_send_timeout 2s; } } TMPL # Substitute only our env vars; leave $hostname and $nginx_version for nginx envsubst '${SERVER_ID} ${CLUSTER} ${MESSAGE}' < /tmp/nginx.tmpl > /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf rm /tmp/nginx.tmpl nginx -g 'daemon off;' & NGINX_PID=$! sleep 2 # Hit the dead upstream 15 times to pre-fill the error log with connection failures for i in $(seq 1 15); do wget -q -O /dev/null http://localhost/app 2>/dev/null || true done wait $NGINX_PID ", "ls -la /app/logs/server-c2": "total 16 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 May 12 01:55 . drwxr-xr-x 6 root root 4096 May 12 01:55 .. -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 501 May 12 02:01 access.log -rw-r--r-- 1 root root 1836 May 12 02:01 error.log ", "GET /status": "HTTP 200 — {"server_id":"server-c2","cluster":"C","hostname":"server-c2","app_status":"ok","message":"I am a cluster C server!","nginx":{"version":"1.26.2","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}", "GET /app": "HTTP 502 — 502 Bad Gateway

502 Bad Gateway

nginx/1.26.2
" }, "server-c3": { "files": "entrypoint.sh server-c3.json", "entrypoint.sh": "#!/bin/sh set -e envsubst '${SERVER_ID} ${CLUSTER} ${MESSAGE}' \ < /etc/nginx/templates/default.conf.template \ > /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf nginx -g 'daemon off;' & NGINX_PID=$! sleep 1 # Repeatedly kill nginx worker processes (children of the master). # The master automatically respawns them, creating a visible crash loop in ps aux. (while kill -0 $NGINX_PID 2>/dev/null; do sleep 8 for pid in $(ls /proc/ 2>/dev/null); do [ -f /proc/$pid/stat ] || continue ppid=$(awk '{print $4}' /proc/$pid/stat 2>/dev/null) [ "$ppid" = "$NGINX_PID" ] && kill "$pid" 2>/dev/null || true done done) & wait $NGINX_PID ", "ls -la /app/logs/server-c3": "total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 May 9 14:08 . drwxr-xr-x 6 root root 4096 May 12 01:55 .. lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 access.log -> /dev/stdout lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 error.log -> /dev/stderr ", "GET /status": "HTTP 200 — {"server_id":"server-c3","cluster":"C","hostname":"server-c3","app_status":"ok","message":"I am a cluster C server!","nginx":{"version":"1.26.2","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}", "GET /app": "HTTP 404 — 404 Not Found

nginx/1.26.2
" }, "server-c4": { "files": "nginx.conf.template server-c4.json", "nginx.conf.template": "upstream app_backend { server 127.0.0.1:3000; } server { listen 80; server_name _; default_type application/json; location /status { return 200 '{"server_id":"${SERVER_ID}","cluster":"${CLUSTER}","hostname":"$hostname","app_status":"ok","message":"${MESSAGE}","nginx":{"version":"$nginx_version","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}'; } location /app { proxy_pass http://app_backend; proxy_connect_timeout 5s; proxy_read_timeout 5s; } } ", "ls -la /app/logs/server-c4": "total 8 drwxr-xr-x 2 root root 4096 May 9 14:08 . drwxr-xr-x 6 root root 4096 May 12 01:55 .. lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 access.log -> /dev/stdout lrwxrwxrwx 1 root root 11 Jan 8 2025 error.log -> /dev/stderr ", "GET /status": "HTTP 200 — {"server_id":"server-c4","cluster":"C","hostname":"server-c4","app_status":"ok","message":"I am a cluster C server!","nginx":{"version":"1.26.2","status":"running"},"ports":{"http":80}}", "GET /app": "HTTP 502 — 502 Bad Gateway

502 Bad Gateway

nginx/1.26.2
" } } For each server, list every deviation from the ideal state across all its files. Then, for each deviation, ask: does this change produce harm on its own, or does its effect depend on one or more of the other deviations on this server? Group deviations that only produce harm in combination into a single finding. Name the finding by the net observable harm to users or operators, not by the mechanism that causes it. Before treating any deviation as a standalone finding, ask: if this deviation were removed, would any other deviation on this server become pointless or significantly less harmful? If yes, group them into one finding named by the net harm to users or operators, not by the mechanism. Only report a deviation separately if it produces distinct harm regardless of what else is present on the server. ## Tool constraints read_only_shell allows: cat, grep, ls, tail Shell operators NOT allowed: $, &&, ;, <, >, >>, `, |, ||

function.description:
Run a read-only shell command. Useful for reading config files and scripts mounted at /app/servers/.
Args:
    command: The requested command.
Returns: The result of that command.
function.name: read_only_shell

function.description:
Make a GET request to a named server container by its server_id (e.g. 'server-c1'). 
Returns HTTP status and response body. Use path='/status' or path='/app'.
Args: 
    server_id: id of relevant server.
    path: path of server.
Returns:
    Result of GET request.
function.name: http_probe

function.description:
Run a read-only shell command. Useful for reading config files and scripts mounted at /app/servers/.
Args:
    command: The requested command.
Returns: The result of that command.
function.name: read_only_shell

function.description:
Make a GET request to a named server container by its server_id (e.g. 'server-c1'). 
Returns HTTP status and response body. Use path='/status' or path='/app'.
Args: 
    server_id: id of relevant server.
    path: path of server.
Returns:
    Result of GET request.
function.name: http_probe

Group deviations that only produce harm in combination into a single finding. Name the finding by the net observable harm to users or operators, not by the mechanism that causes it. Before treating any deviation as a standalone finding, ask: if this deviation were removed, would any other deviation on this server become pointless or significantly less harmful? If yes, group them into one finding named by the net harm to users or operators, not by the mechanism. function.description:
Run a read-only shell command. Useful for reading config files and scripts mounted at /app/servers/.
Args:
    command: The requested command.
Returns: The result of that command.
function.name: read_only_shell

function.description:
Make a GET request to a named server container by its server_id (e.g. 'server-c1'). 
Returns HTTP status and response body. Use path='/status' or path='/app'.
Args: 
    server_id: id of relevant server.
    path: path of server.
Returns:
    Result of GET request.
function.name: http_probe

aggregated_issues.issue: Nginx continues serving but the active configuration file is deliberately corrupted after startup, making config validation/reload fail. Root cause: server-c1 entrypoint.sh appends an unterminated server block containing an invalid directive to /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf after nginx has started.

The /app endpoint is configured to proxy to an unavailable local backend, so users hitting /app receive an upstream failure. Root cause: server-c2 entrypoint.sh generates an nginx upstream pointing at 127.0.0.1:3000 and a /app proxy_pass to that upstream.

Nginx access and error logs are written to ordinary files inside the container instead of Docker stdout/stderr, reducing operator log visibility and risking unbounded disk growth. Root cause: server-c2 entrypoint.sh removes the Docker log symlinks and configures error_log/access_log to /var/log/nginx files.

Nginx workers are repeatedly killed, creating an ongoing worker crash/respawn loop and intermittent service instability. Root cause: server-c3 entrypoint.sh starts a background loop that identifies children of the nginx master and kills them every 8 seconds.

The /app endpoint is configured to proxy to an unavailable local backend, so users hitting /app receive an upstream failure. Root cause: server-c4 nginx.conf.template defines an nginx upstream pointing at 127.0.0.1:3000 and a /app proxy_pass to that upstream.

tickets_created.description: server-c1 is currently serving traffic from the configuration loaded at startup, but /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf has been corrupted afterward by the entrypoint script. As a result, nginx configuration validation and reloads fail, preventing safe configuration changes or service reloads and creating operational risk on the next restart/redeploy.
tickets_created.issue: Nginx continues serving but the active configuration file is deliberately corrupted after startup, making config validation/reload fail. Root cause: server-c1 entrypoint.sh appends an unterminated server block containing an invalid directive to /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf after nginx has started.
tickets_created.remediation:
Run this on server-c1 as root. It backs up the files, removes entrypoint lines/heredocs that append to /etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf, restores a valid default server config, validates it, and reloads Nginx. If server-c1 is container/image-built, also apply this entrypoint change in the image/source repo and redeploy so it persists.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

CONF=/etc/nginx/conf.d/default.conf
ENTRYPOINT=${ENTRYPOINT:-}
if [[ -z "$ENTRYPOINT" ]]; then
  for p in /entrypoint.sh /usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh /docker-entrypoint.sh /usr/local/bin/docker-entrypoint.sh; do
    [[ -f "$p" ]] && ENTRYPOINT="$p" && break
  done
fi
[[ -f "$ENTRYPOINT" ]] || { echo "Set ENTRYPOINT=/path/to/entrypoint.sh and rerun" >&2; exit 1; }

ts=$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)
cp -a "$ENTRYPOINT" "$ENTRYPOINT.bak.$ts"
cp -a "$CONF" "$CONF.bak.$ts"

tmp=$(mktemp)
skip=0
delim=
while IFS= read -r line || [[ -n "$line" ]]; do
  if [[ "$skip" -eq 1 ]]; then
    trimmed=$(printf '%s\n' "$line" | sed 's/^[[:space:]]*//;s/[[:space:]]*$//')
    [[ "$trimmed" == "$delim" ]] && skip=0
    continue
  fi

  if [[ "$line" == *"$CONF"* && ( "$line" == *">>"* || "$line" =~ tee[[:space:]]+-a ) ]]; then
    if [[ "$line" == *"<<"* ]]; then
      delim=${line#*<<}
      delim=${delim#-}
      delim=$(printf '%s\n' "$delim" | awk '{print $1}' | tr -d "'\"")
      skip=1
    fi
    continue
  fi

  printf '%s\n' "$line" >> "$tmp"
done < "$ENTRYPOINT"
cat "$tmp" > "$ENTRYPOINT"
rm -f "$tmp"
chmod +x "$ENTRYPOINT"

cat > "$CONF" <<'NGINX'
server {
    listen 80 default_server;
    listen [::]:80 default_server;
    server_name _;
    root /usr/share/nginx/html;
    index index.html index.htm;

    location / {
        try_files $uri $uri/ =404;
    }
}
NGINX

nginx -t
nginx -s reload 2>/dev/null || systemctl reload nginx
```

tickets_created.description: The /app route on server-c2 is returning upstream failures because Nginx is generated with a proxy target of 127.0.0.1:3000, but no backend is available at that local address. This makes the /app endpoint unavailable for users routed to this server.
tickets_created.issue: The /app endpoint is configured to proxy to an unavailable local backend, so users hitting /app receive an upstream failure. Root cause: server-c2 entrypoint.sh generates an nginx upstream pointing at 127.0.0.1:3000 and a /app proxy_pass to that upstream.
tickets_created.remediation:
Fix the generated Nginx upstream so /app points to the actual reachable backend, then reload Nginx. Example Bash remediation for server-c2, using environment variables for the correct backend host/port:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Set these to the real backend service for /app before running.
: "${APP_BACKEND_HOST:?Set APP_BACKEND_HOST to the reachable /app backend host}"
: "${APP_BACKEND_PORT:?Set APP_BACKEND_PORT to the reachable /app backend port}"

ENTRYPOINT="/entrypoint.sh"

# Back up current entrypoint.
cp "$ENTRYPOINT" "${ENTRYPOINT}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"

# Replace the hard-coded unavailable local upstream with the real backend.
sed -i -E "s/127\.0\.0\.1:3000/${APP_BACKEND_HOST}:${APP_BACKEND_PORT}/g" "$ENTRYPOINT"

# Regenerate Nginx config by re-running the entrypoint if that is the deployment pattern,
# or restart the service/container that runs this entrypoint.
# Then validate and reload Nginx.
nginx -t
nginx -s reload

# Verify the endpoint no longer returns an upstream failure.
curl -fsS -I http://127.0.0.1/app
```

If /app is intended to be served by a local process instead, start and enable that backend on 127.0.0.1:3000, then run `nginx -t && nginx -s reload` and verify with `curl -fsS -I http://127.0.0.1/app`.

tickets_created.description: server-c2 is bypassing Docker-native log collection for Nginx. Its entrypoint removes the standard Docker log symlinks and configures Nginx access/error logs to ordinary files under /var/log/nginx. Operators may miss important request/error events in `docker logs` or the platform log pipeline, and the container filesystem can grow without bound if those files are not rotated.
tickets_created.issue: Nginx access and error logs are written to ordinary files inside the container instead of Docker stdout/stderr, reducing operator log visibility and risking unbounded disk growth. Root cause: server-c2 entrypoint.sh removes the Docker log symlinks and configures error_log/access_log to /var/log/nginx files.
tickets_created.remediation:
Update server-c2's Nginx container entrypoint/config so access logs go to stdout and error logs go to stderr. Remove any entrypoint logic that deletes Docker log symlinks.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Run on server-c2 against the affected Nginx container/image workspace.
# Set these paths if they differ in your deployment.
ENTRYPOINT="./entrypoint.sh"
NGINX_CONF="./nginx.conf"
DEFAULT_CONF="./conf.d/default.conf"

# 1) Stop removing Docker log symlinks from the entrypoint.
if [[ -f "$ENTRYPOINT" ]]; then
  cp "$ENTRYPOINT" "${ENTRYPOINT}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
  sed -i -E '\#rm[[:space:]-f]+/var/log/nginx/(access|error)\.log#d' "$ENTRYPOINT"
  sed -i -E '\#unlink[[:space:]]+/var/log/nginx/(access|error)\.log#d' "$ENTRYPOINT"

  # Ensure the symlinks are present when the container starts.
  if ! grep -q 'ln -sf /dev/stdout /var/log/nginx/access.log' "$ENTRYPOINT"; then
    sed -i '/^[[:space:]]*exec /i \
mkdir -p /var/log/nginx\
ln -sf /dev/stdout /var/log/nginx/access.log\
ln -sf /dev/stderr /var/log/nginx/error.log\
' "$ENTRYPOINT"
  fi
fi

# 2) Configure Nginx to emit directly to Docker stdout/stderr.
for conf in "$NGINX_CONF" "$DEFAULT_CONF"; do
  [[ -f "$conf" ]] || continue
  cp "$conf" "${conf}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
  sed -i -E 's#error_log[[:space:]]+[^;]+;#error_log /dev/stderr warn;#g' "$conf"
  sed -i -E 's#access_log[[:space:]]+[^;]+;#access_log /dev/stdout;#g' "$conf"
done

# 3) Validate before rollout. Run inside the rebuilt/running container as appropriate.
nginx -t
nginx -s reload
```

After redeploying/restarting the container, verify:

```bash
docker logs <nginx_container_name_or_id> --tail 100
# Confirm access/error events appear here.

docker exec <nginx_container_name_or_id> sh -c 'ls -l /var/log/nginx/access.log /var/log/nginx/error.log'
# Expected: access.log -> /dev/stdout and error.log -> /dev/stderr, or Nginx config directly references /dev/stdout and /dev/stderr.
```

tickets_created.description: server-c3 is running an entrypoint script that continuously kills Nginx worker processes. The Nginx master process respawns workers, but the background loop kills them again every 8 seconds, causing repeated worker crashes and intermittent service instability for traffic handled by this server.
tickets_created.issue: Nginx workers are repeatedly killed, creating an ongoing worker crash/respawn loop and intermittent service instability. Root cause: server-c3 entrypoint.sh starts a background loop that identifies children of the nginx master and kills them every 8 seconds.
tickets_created.remediation:
Remove the intentional worker-killing loop from `entrypoint.sh`, redeploy the corrected entrypoint, and restart the service/container so Nginx workers are no longer terminated.

Run on `server-c3`:

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

ENTRYPOINT="/entrypoint.sh"

if [[ ! -f "$ENTRYPOINT" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: $ENTRYPOINT not found. Locate the active entrypoint.sh and update ENTRYPOINT accordingly." >&2
  exit 1
fi

BACKUP="${ENTRYPOINT}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
cp -a "$ENTRYPOINT" "$BACKUP"
echo "Backed up $ENTRYPOINT to $BACKUP"

# Remove the known bad background loop that kills Nginx worker children every 8 seconds.
# This targets blocks containing: while/sleep loop + nginx master child discovery + kill.
python3 - <<'PY'
from pathlib import Path
import re

path = Path('/entrypoint.sh')
text = path.read_text()

patterns = [
    # Common one-line or compact background loop form.
    re.compile(r'''\n?\s*\(?\s*while\s+true\s*;?\s*do\s*.*?(?:pgrep|ps).*?(?:nginx).*?(?:master).*?kill.*?sleep\s+8\s*;?\s*done\s*\)?\s*&\s*\n?''', re.I | re.S),
    # Multi-line loop where kill occurs before sleep 8.
    re.compile(r'''\n?\s*while\s+true\s*;?\s*do\s*\n(?:(?!done).)*?(?:pgrep|ps).*(?:nginx|master).*\n(?:(?!done).)*?kill.*\n(?:(?!done).)*?sleep\s+8\s*\n\s*done\s*&?\s*\n?''', re.I | re.S),
]

new = text
for pat in patterns:
    new = pat.sub('\n# Removed bad loop: do not kill nginx workers from entrypoint.sh\n', new)

if new == text:
    raise SystemExit('ERROR: No matching nginx worker-killing loop was removed. Manually remove the loop that finds nginx master children and kills them every 8 seconds, then rerun validation.')

path.write_text(new)
PY

chmod +x "$ENTRYPOINT"

# Validate that the bad behavior is gone from the entrypoint.
if grep -Eiq 'kill|pkill|pgrep|sleep 8|nginx.*master' "$ENTRYPOINT"; then
  echo "Review $ENTRYPOINT to ensure no remaining loop kills nginx workers:" >&2
  grep -Ein 'kill|pkill|pgrep|sleep 8|nginx.*master' "$ENTRYPOINT" >&2 || true
fi

# Restart the affected service. Use the command appropriate for this host.
if systemctl list-unit-files | grep -q '^nginx\.service'; then
  systemctl restart nginx
  systemctl status nginx --no-pager
elif command -v docker >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  # Replace nginx_container_name if needed.
  docker ps --format '{{.Names}}' | grep -E '^nginx|nginx' || true
  echo "If Nginx is containerized, rebuild/recreate the container image with the fixed entrypoint and restart it, for example:"
  echo "  docker compose up -d --build nginx"
else
  nginx -t && nginx -s reload
fi

# Confirm workers remain stable for longer than the previous 8-second kill interval.
echo "Validating Nginx worker stability for 30 seconds..."
ps -eo pid,ppid,comm,args | grep '[n]ginx'
sleep 30
ps -eo pid,ppid,comm,args | grep '[n]ginx'
echo "Remediation complete if worker PIDs are not being killed/recreated every 8 seconds and logs show no worker exits caused by SIGTERM/SIGKILL from entrypoint.sh."
```

tickets_created.description: On cluster C, server-c4 has an nginx /app route that proxies to an upstream defined as 127.0.0.1:3000. That local backend is unavailable, so requests to /app fail with an upstream/proxy error for users hitting this endpoint.
tickets_created.issue: The /app endpoint is configured to proxy to an unavailable local backend, so users hitting /app receive an upstream failure. Root cause: server-c4 nginx.conf.template defines an nginx upstream pointing at 127.0.0.1:3000 and a /app proxy_pass to that upstream.
tickets_created.remediation:
Because the nginx root cause is known, correct the /app upstream so it points to a reachable backend, then validate and reload nginx. Replace `APP_BACKEND_HOST` and `APP_BACKEND_PORT` with the actual application backend for /app.

```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Run on server-c4.
# Set these to the real, reachable /app backend.
APP_BACKEND_HOST="<REPLACE_WITH_BACKEND_HOST>"
APP_BACKEND_PORT="<REPLACE_WITH_BACKEND_PORT>"
NGINX_TEMPLATE="/etc/nginx/nginx.conf.template"

if [[ "$APP_BACKEND_HOST" == "<REPLACE_WITH_BACKEND_HOST>" || "$APP_BACKEND_PORT" == "<REPLACE_WITH_BACKEND_PORT>" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: Set APP_BACKEND_HOST and APP_BACKEND_PORT before running." >&2
  exit 1
fi

sudo cp "$NGINX_TEMPLATE" "${NGINX_TEMPLATE}.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"

# Replace the unavailable local upstream target.
sudo sed -i -E "s#127\.0\.0\.1:3000#${APP_BACKEND_HOST}:${APP_BACKEND_PORT}#g" "$NGINX_TEMPLATE"

# If nginx is generated from the template by a deployment/render step, run that step here.
# Otherwise, apply the same replacement to the active nginx config if present.
if [[ -f /etc/nginx/nginx.conf ]]; then
  sudo cp /etc/nginx/nginx.conf "/etc/nginx/nginx.conf.bak.$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
  sudo sed -i -E "s#127\.0\.0\.1:3000#${APP_BACKEND_HOST}:${APP_BACKEND_PORT}#g" /etc/nginx/nginx.conf
fi

sudo nginx -t
sudo systemctl reload nginx

curl -fIs http://127.0.0.1/app >/dev/null && echo "/app is reachable through nginx"
```

If /app is supposed to be served by a local process instead, deploy/start that service so it listens on `127.0.0.1:3000`, then run `sudo nginx -t && sudo systemctl reload nginx` and verify with `curl -fI http://127.0.0.1/app`.
